openalex.title: CDK12Loss Promotes Prostate Cancer Development While Exposing Vulnerabilities to Paralog-Based Synthetic Lethality
openalex.display_name: CDK12Loss Promotes Prostate Cancer Development While Exposing Vulnerabilities to Paralog-Based Synthetic Lethality

locations.raw_source_name: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology

crossref.abstract: <jats:title>SUMMARY</jats:title><jats:p>Biallelic loss of cyclin-dependent kinase 12 (<jats:italic>CDK12</jats:italic>) defines a unique molecular subtype of metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). It remains unclear, however, whether<jats:italic>CDK12</jats:italic>loss<jats:italic>per se</jats:italic>is sufficient to drive prostate cancer development—either alone, or in the context of other genetic alterations—and whether<jats:italic>CDK12</jats:italic>-mutant tumors exhibit sensitivity to specific pharmacotherapies. Here, we demonstrate that tissue-specific<jats:italic>Cdk12</jats:italic>ablation is sufficient to induce preneoplastic lesions and robust T cell infiltration in the mouse prostate. Allograft-based CRISPR screening demonstrated that<jats:italic>Cdk12</jats:italic>loss is positively associated with<jats:italic>Trp53</jats:italic>inactivation but negatively associated with<jats:italic>Pten</jats:italic>inactivation—akin to what is observed in human mCRPC. Consistent with this, ablation of<jats:italic>Cdk12</jats:italic>in prostate organoids with concurrent<jats:italic>Trp53</jats:italic>loss promotes their proliferation and ability to form tumors in mice, while<jats:italic>Cdk12</jats:italic>knockout in the<jats:italic>Pten</jats:italic>-null prostate cancer mouse model abrogates tumor growth. Bigenic<jats:italic>Cdk12</jats:italic>and<jats:italic>Trp53</jats:italic>loss allografts represent a new syngeneic model for the study of androgen receptor (AR)-positive, luminal prostate cancer. Notably,<jats:italic>Cdk12/Trp53</jats:italic>loss prostate tumors are sensitive to immune checkpoint blockade.<jats:italic>Cdk12</jats:italic>-null organoids (either with or without<jats:italic>Trp53</jats:italic>co-ablation) and patient-derived xenografts from tumors with<jats:italic>CDK12</jats:italic>inactivation are highly sensitive to inhibition or degradation of its paralog kinase, CDK13. Together, these data identify<jats:italic>CDK12</jats:italic>as a<jats:italic>bona fide</jats:italic>tumor suppressor gene with impact on tumor progression and lends support to paralog-based synthetic lethality as a promising strategy for treating<jats:italic>CDK12-</jats:italic>mutant mCRPC.</jats:p>
title: CDK12Loss Promotes Prostate Cancer Development While Exposing Vulnerabilities to Paralog-Based Synthetic Lethality

reference.article-title: Development of seven new human prostate tumor xenograft models and their histopathological characterization

reference.unstructured: Chang, Y. , Wang, X. , Yang, J. , Tien, J.C.-Y. , Mannan, R. , Zhang, Y. , Magnuson, B. , Mahapatra, S. , Wang, C. , Wang, Z. , et al. (Manuscript in preparation). Development of an orally bioavailable CDK12/13 degrader and induction of synthetic lethality with AKT pathway inhibition.
